data.title: Conagra Brands: Don't Rely On This Consumer Defensive For Market Protection
data.description:
Investment Thesis

To say the last few months have been volatile for the stock market would be an understatement. Investors are grappling with the market volatility from major events like “Liberation Day” and increasing geopolitical conflicts.

Investors might assume that Conagra Brands, Inc. (NYSE:CAG), a consumer durable business with a 6.5% dividend yield and recent stock price drop of 28%, now makes it a perfect investment for market protection – but investors would be mistaken.

Conagra Brands has an increasingly weak financial position – a low cash balance with a large debt burden and growing interest expenses. Its operations have similarly deteriorated like its financial position. The company has experienced almost no revenue growth in the two years following its 2018 acquisition of Pinnacle Foods and is still suffering from the supply chain and restructuring costs of the large integration.

I am initiating a Sell rating with an estimated share price of $19.90 and recommend diligent investors avoid the lure of a high dividend yield and the perceived safety attached to a company operating in the consumer defensive sector.

Company Overview

Market Cap

$9.76B

5yr Revenue Growth

2.5%

Enterprise Value

$17.86B

Operating Margin

14.9%

12-month Return (%)

-28.0%

Net Margin

2.8%

Inside Ownership

0.28%

5yr. Dividend CAGR

11.4%

Conagra Brands operates on a fiscal year that ends in May – it will report on its year-end results for 2025 on July 10th, two weeks from now. The company operates an assortment of well-known consumer durable brands with a large shelf presence at most grocery stores across the United States.

Conagra Brands leverages its strong brand presence and shelf space to generate substantial free cash flow – the 2025 TTM free cash flow yield is 13.5% as of the most recent quarter’s results.

A consumer durable business primarily uses its free cash flow to acquire new businesses (inorganic growth through acquisitions), repurchase shares, pay investors a dividend, or pay down debt.

Conagra Brands used its free cash flow to make its most important acquisition in the last decade - the 2018 purchase of Pinnacle Foods. The acquisition was financed through debt and new shares of common stock. The company issued approximately 24% more outstanding shares to finance the acquisition.

Conagra has continued to use its free cash flow to make new acquisitions since its purchase of Pinnacle, but the company has not pursued a strategic share repurchase program. I believe a lack of share repurchases and the issuance of company stock to finance acquisitions implies that Conagra’s management does not believe the company’s share price is undervalued enough to repurchase its common stock.

The company has deployed most of its free cash flow in dividends – the dividend was cut in 2006 but has since grown at a compound annual growth rate of 5.2% through 2024. However, most of the dividend growth came immediately after the Pinnacle acquisition.

Given the five-year stock price decline of 37% and the increase in dividends, investors now have the opportunity to purchase Conagra with a dividend yield of 6.5%, the highest yield in company history. But sustainability and growth of the dividend are far more important than the current yield for diligent investors with a medium to long-term investment horizon, and the balance sheet as of the end of the company’s third quarter 2025 should give investors concern.

At the time of the third quarter earnings call, Conagra had $49.4M of cash on hand, down from $553.3M during mid-2020. The decrease in cash is primarily driven by the large debt servicing of the Pinnacle Foods acquisition and the spike in dividend payments immediately following the acquisition.

Given the company's low liquidity, its large debt balance creates a concern – with over $7.2B in long-term debt as of Q3 ’25, the yearly interest expense is $430M and growing.

Additionally, some long-term notes, held at very advantageous rates, are set to expire soon. Take, for example, the $1B 2027 note at 1.375% - refinancing this note at current rates for a BBB- company would increase Conagra’s yearly interest expense by over $50M.

As more long-term debt at lower weighted average costs of capital comes due, Conagra will be forced to refinance at higher rates. Instead of advantageously using its free cash flow to pay down debt, it continues to issue a growing dividend for its shareholders.

Although I like growing dividends, I like safe and reliable dividends even more – but with the interest expense set to increase from upcoming refinancing needs, and the company’s cash balance near an all-time low, it will become more difficult for management to sustain or increase its existing dividend in my opinion.

The interest on long-term debt is not the only expense I’m worried about – Conagra’s balance sheet shows accumulated depreciation on property, plant & equipment of over 57%. Accumulated depreciation on equipment in the range of 50% to 70% is a good indicator that expenses will increase in the foreseeable future to account for the aging of equipment.

Just like the interest expense, future repair and replacement of equipment, or ‘capital expenditures’, will be higher under an inflationary environment compared to the near-zero rate world prior to 2022. The increase in forecasted expenses over the next several years will likely squeeze Conagra’s free cash flow, making it more difficult for the company to sustain its dividend and pay down debt.

Operations

As a branded consumer packaged goods company, Conagra’s best operational opportunities are inorganic growth (through acquisitions that complement its core offerings) and cost control.

Conagra’s purchase of Pinnacle, mentioned previously, provided strong top-line inorganic growth to the Grocery & Snacks and Refrigerated & Frozen segments of the business in 2019 and 2020. That growth was not sustained – from 2021 through the third quarter of 2025, revenue has only grown at a compound annual growth rate of ~1.2%.

Previous earnings calls have made clear that revenue growth in the last few years has mostly been driven by the macroeconomic headwind in the form of inflationary price raises – not through increased sales volume.

Despite the top-line performance difficulties, management embarked on a multi-year cost efficiency program following the Pinnacle acquisition in an attempt to decrease sales, general, and administrative expenses at the corporate level. Without strong cost control, margins can erode over time, especially following large acquisitions.

Since the announcement of the program in 2019, Conagra has spent over $286M in restructuring costs, supply chain upgrades, etc. to address cost inefficiencies. I am not confident in the results. From 2022 to 2024, sales, general, and administrative expenses increased by 66%, per the SEC annual report filings.

Conagra’s operational performance over the last several years draws concern. The weak top-line growth and inability to reign in core expenses have led to unsatisfactory results.

Valuation

I have implemented a discounted cash flow model to value Conagra and have included assumptions on the projected income statement taken from management expectations, historical performance, and macroeconomic considerations.

Although I have spoken fairly unfavorably of the company’s operational and financial performance, I expect a modest decrease in expenses driven by supply chain normalization and declining inflation. However, I expect most of the cost gains to be offset by declining revenue – I have projected a 2% sequential revenue decline until 2027, the terminal year used in my model.

I have incorporated a weighted average cost of capital using Bruce Greenwald's discount value approach (Greenwald, B. C., Kahn, J. (2011). Value Investing: From Graham to Buffett and Beyond), which focuses on volatility-agnostic underlying business risk. In this scenario, I have used a fixed 8% cost of capital based on Conagra's debt-to-equity structure and terminal growth rate of 1%.

Forecasting slight decreases in the cost of goods sold, and interest expense benefits the net margin substantially. After finishing 2024 with a 2.9% net margin, projecting a 550bps increase is very generous but is more in line with the company’s historical operating performance.

Ultimately, I have arrived at an implied share price of $19.90 and reiterate my Sell rating on Conagra.

There is currently no price scenario that I would find reasonable to make an investment in the company. However, for investors interested in Conagra, I recommend monitoring the company for key changes in operational and financial improvements.

Conagra must find accretive acquisitions that support long-term revenue growth, unlike the recent results from the Pinnacle acquisition. Additionally, the company must obtain better control over its restructuring and sales, general, and administrative costs.

More efforts should be taken to reduce the significant debt burden, and subsequent yearly interest expense, to de-leverage the balance sheet. As mentioned previously, the long-term debt maturity expirations that come due in the next few years will add substantial interest expense, further reducing margins and available free cash flow.

Risks

I have identified two risks to my thesis that could lead to a rating upgrade - one is company-specific while the other relies on positive macroeconomic changes.

First, I have assumed that management will continue to allocate capital in the same manner as in the last few years. If management were to use a larger portion of its extensive free cash flow to pay down higher-yield debt, the company's deleveraged balance sheet would look much stronger.

Financial deleveraging would also greatly benefit operating and net margins from interest expense reduction. For example, if the 2025 forecasted interest expense were to halve, the net margin would increase by 120bps based on my projected income statement shown previously.

The second risk to my bearish thesis is the macroeconomic picture - if household spending were to increase from a reduction in interest rates, Conagra's sales volume would likely increase as robust consumer spending behavior returns. Conagra's valuation would look substantially different if sales volume drove overall revenue growth by, for example, a 3% compound annual growth rate compared with my -2% growth rate.

Conclusion

Consumer durable businesses with high free cash flow generation that reward investors with substantial dividends are great investments during periods of market volatility. Although Conagra currently boasts a 6.5% dividend yield and contains a portfolio of consumer durable businesses, the company carries significant risk.

Conagra’s weak balance sheet – high levels of debt with very little cash on hand – should leave disciplined investors concerned over the safety of future dividend sustainability and growth. Operationally, revenue growth has been outpaced by inflation while the company’s cost controls have not led to a decrease in operating expenses.

I have initiated a Sell rating on Conagra with an implied share price of $19.90 and recommend investors looking for high dividends and business durability consider alternate opportunities.

data.title: Ally Financial: Shares Look Cheap On Easing Cyclical Headwinds
data.description:
Ally Financial (NYSE:ALLY) hasn't enjoyed the best of times of late. On the business front, Detroit-based Ally's recent results are something of a mess, with the firm booking a one-off charge that plunged it into the red last quarter. Its underlying numbers aren't so great either, with the business struggling to earn its cost of equity on the back of some usual cycle-related headwinds. As a result, shares of the car finance giant have lagged the wider U.S. financials space by nearly 30 points over the past 12 months.

I am inclined to think that represents an opportunity for today's prospective buyer. Ally's core results aren't great, but that is par for the course given the cyclical nature of its business. In exchange, shares only trade for 7.2x 2026 consensus EPS – a multiple that I expect will lead to a solid return over the next couple of years.

Earnings Subdued By Typical Cyclical Issues

Ally's asset base skews heavily to retail auto loans. There's over $80 billion worth of them on its balance sheet, mapping to a circa 45% share of its total interest-earning assets. Commercial auto is another $22 billion, or 12%. Even in the context of the wider banking industry, I would say that makes Ally particularly sensitive to the ups and downs of the economic cycle.

Ally's recent financials are admittedly quite messy. Technically, it made a loss of $253 million for its common stockholders last quarter, or $0.82 per share. However, this was distorted by a major one-off, with the bank booking a $495 million pretax loss due to a repositioning of its securities portfolio. Essentially, Ally sold a chunk of low-yielding bonds and repurchased them at prevailing rates, increasing its forward earnings power.

Still, core results are also quite weak. Excluding the above, Ally made around $180 million in net profit for the first quarter. This mapped to a core return on tangible common equity (ROTCE) of just 8.3% using management's definition. Looking back, Ally has averaged a circa 13%-14% core ROTCE in recent times, and it has cleared a double-digit ROTCE in 24 out of the last 30 quarters.

Clearly, the firm is under-earning right now, and this has been caused by typical cycle-related issues. On the revenue side, Ally's lending margin has been squeezed by the industry-wide rise in funding costs, with the bank's reported net interest margin (NIM) clocking in at 3.3% last quarter. This figure is around 20bps below its 5-year average.

The situation is slowly improving. Short-end interest rates have fallen from their peak, and the associated decline in Ally's cost of funds has outpaced the decline in its earning-asset yield. Its NIM was up around 15bps year-on-year last quarter and should continue to improve throughout 2025 and 2026.

Looking at some drivers of that, Ally has a further $26 billion in term deposits maturating over the rest of the year, including around $11 billion this quarter. Per below, the blended yield on its maturing CDs is comfortably over 4%, which is well above Ally's prevailing offering to customers. Management expects full-year NIM of 3.40%-3.50% this year, which also implies a strong comp in 2026.

Credit quality has been another pressing issue for Ally. Delinquencies and net charge-offs are up from their COVID lows, influenced by normalizing used-car prices, which forms the collateral that backs much of its loan book.

There are encouraging signs on this front, too. Net charge-offs landed at 2.12% in the Retail auto-book last quarter, an improvement of 15bps year-on-year. While losses remain elevated compared to their historical average, there is tangible evidence of tighter underwriting standards, with more recent vintages displaying better credit metrics. This should power ongoing improvement in Ally's asset quality in the coming quarters.

As a result of these twin issues, Ally's risk-adjusted interest revenue has been squeezed. Specifically, loan loss provisioning consumed nearly 40% of its net interest revenue last quarter, which is around 15ppt above its five-year average.

Shares Look Cheap

Like all cyclical stocks, timing the rebound is obviously a key consideration, as these issues have plagued Ally for a number of quarters now. However, with NIM expanding and asset quality improving earnings could be about to turn for the firm – a sentiment that is seemingly shared by the sell-side analysts covering the stock. Per Seeking Alpha, they see Ally's quarterly EPS exceeding the $1 mark in the near term, leading to around $5.40 per share in annual EPS by 2026.

Ally trades for $38.78 as I type. The stock does not require an aggressive multiple of these earnings to drive attractive returns. Indeed, a trailing price/earnings ratio of less than 9x would support double-digit annualized share price appreciation over the next couple of years. Including its $0.30 per share quarterly dividend, I expect Ally's total return to comfortably clear its cost of equity.

As an aside, this should also provide a nice tailwind to Ally's capital levels. Currently, Ally's pro forma Common Equity Tier 1 ratio is 9.7%, but this falls to around 7.5% including AOCI losses. That is a little thin for my liking, with the latter only being around 40bps above its required level. As Ally's earnings normalize, its ratio of net income over risk-weighted assets will also improve. Put simply, that will give the firm more scope to build capital, and this will be boosted by the ongoing improvement in its AOCI losses.

Conclusion

Ally Financial has been a relatively weak performer in recent times, as the firm continues to grapple with cyclical headwinds that have squeezed its earnings. Positively, there is tangible evidence that relief is on the horizon, with its NIM expanding and credit quality showing tentative signs of improvement. In turn, this should drive meaningful growth in Ally's earnings in the coming quarters. On a relatively modest multiple, this can power an attractive total return for today's investors. Accordingly, I rate the shares a Buy.

data.title: Compass Diversified: Preferred Stock Is No Less Risky
data.description:
It's not often that I look at a preferred stock Compass Diversified 7.875 CUM PFD C (NYSE:CODI.PR.C), but when it gives a good yield like 10%, it's hard not to be interested, at least initially. Still, Compass Diversified (CODI) has had problems.

The stock is still down somewhat, after taking a plunge in May when the Board of Directors announced an internal investigation of the company's finances. While I think it's more likely the dividends continue to get paid, the not-so-small possibility of bankruptcy deters me from rating it a Buy.

The Disputed Financials

Compass Diversified is a holding company that invests its capital by acquiring small and middle-market businesses. The current holdings (and their business) are:

  • BOA: Footwear
  • Lugano: Jewelry
  • PrimaLoft: Insulation in outerwear
  • The Honey Pot Co.: Feminine hygiene products
  • Velocity Outdoor: Archery and hunting
  • Altor Solutions: Protective foam products
  • Arnold: Magnets and motors
  • Sterno: Food warming, lighting, home fragrance

This is a lot of positions, implying the benefits of diversification. Of course, it's not an even spread.

The table above shows us that, far and away, Lugano is reported to be the biggest contributor to the consolidated financial results. Yet, as the Form 8K that I cited earlier indicated, Lugano was the company being investigated. Preliminary findings concluded that the 2024 financial reports were no longer reliable. It was later reported that the same conclusions were reached for its 2022 and 2023 reports.

As the other holdings operate independently, however, the investigation does not concern them. It's only the financial impact of Lugano that's been under dispute, pertaining to its own accounting practices, namely "sales, cost of sales, inventory, and accounts receivable."

How the Preferred Dividend Fits

As the main concern is with the dividend, let's consider the company's ability to keep paying it.

So far, it hasn't missed, paying its latest distribution in April.

While the reports were said not to be reliable, it's also true that the problems are specific to Lugano, and we can still glean some things from the rest of the consolidated results.

As it currently stands, the entire preferred capitalization (of which the Series C is the most junior) only needs about $25.4M in earnings to meet its preferred dividend obligations.

Recalling that Lugano contributed 21.4% of revenue in 2024 and 57.6% of operating income, that suggests $1,727M in revenue that isn't compromised by the Lugano probe, as well as $97.5M in operating income.

The company only barely has a controlling interest in Lugano, so much more of the remaining operating income is still attributable to the holding company, as opposed to noncontrolling interests.

Where it gets tricky is farther down the income statement, with interest expense exceeding $100M. This narrowly eclipses the operating income that isn't attributable to Lugano. This led the company to adopt measures to improve liquidity, such as suspending the common dividend and entering forbearance with one of its lenders.

Based on that, it seems like the earnings are just on the threshold of either being able to pay the preferred dividends or not. What do we make of that, going forward?

Future Outlook

As the debt issue goes, however, I am mostly not concerned about that. Consider the maturity schedule, for one.

Relatively little of the long-term debt is due in the next couple of years, which I figure is sufficient for the company to sort out the reporting irregularities and figure out what to do with Lugano.

Even if there is a blip in some dividends, the Series C is cumulative, and so these dividends can be recovered in time.

Where I do get concerned is the covenants of the 2022 term loan. The 2024 Form 10-K elaborates:

A breach of any of these covenants will be an event of default under the 2022 Credit Facility. Upon the occurrence of an event of default under the 2022 Credit Facility, the 2022 Revolving Credit Facility may be terminated, and all outstanding loans and other obligations under the 2022 Credit Facility may become immediately due and payable and any letters of credit then outstanding may be required to be cash collateralized, and the Agent and the Lenders may exercise any rights or remedies available to them under the 2022 Credit Facility. Any such event would materially impair the Company’s ability to conduct its business.

This is why there is a forbearance agreement. It's to avoid an immediate default and bankruptcy. If resolving the issues with Lugano results in financial data that violates the covenants, bankruptcy is an imminent threat.

While it appears that the company could liquidate positively, about half the reported assets are goodwill or intangibles. This leaves only about $2B to cover the liabilities, and we know that some tangible assets may have been overstated by Lugano. While the cumulative feature protects against a missed dividend, there's nothing to protect against bankruptcy.

Conclusion

Do I think bankruptcy is particularly likely in this case? No. The company could sell some assets, and maybe even issue more common shares, all while renegotiating the loan at higher interest. It's the fact that bankruptcy is possible that I'm worried about. While I don't write short theses, there's not an appealing buy case here either. For that reason, I give the Series C a Hold rating.

data.title: Enphase: After Rain Comes Sunshine
data.description:
Investor sentiment towards Enphase Energy, Inc. (NASDAQ:ENPH) remains muted in a difficult market environment. Since my previous article, the stock has seen another steep drop. However, Enphase is not the only solar stock that has struggled in 2025. The new proposed US budget bill shows why it is not easy to love solar stocks. If high interest rates and solar panel tariffs were not already enough to consumers, the removal of residential tax credits starting at the end of 2025 could be another hard pill to swallow. Nonetheless, Enphase remains the king of profitability in the stumbling solar sector.

Enphase's operational performance under a magnifying glass

In the graph below, it is visible that Enphase's stock is to some extent fairly punished because of its declining microinverter shipments, which account for the largest share of revenue. In the last two quarters, year-over-year growth returned to microinverter shipments. The following quarters will be important to get full visibility of possible sustainable growth. In the battery segment, the company is performing at record high levels driven by strong demand.

Over the years, there have been some shifts in Enphase's geographical revenue. The main growth stopper for Enphase was interest rates. International revenue is largely impacted by Europe. The United States increased interest rates a lot quicker than Europe, therefore Europe's growth ended two quarters later. Despite the fact that Europe has been cutting rates more aggressively, the solar market has not seen a recovery yet. On the other hand, the US market showed some progress.

Surprisingly, Enphase was able to maintain their strong gross profit and net profit margin, while others in the industry like SolarEdge Technologies, Inc. (SEDG) and SMA Solar Technology AG (OTCPK:SMTGF) saw their margins evaporate. This showcases the strong management team and their focus on efficiencies.

It is good to know that Enphase did receive a US-based advantage which supported their profit margin. The company is eligible for the advanced manufacturing tax credits (45X). This benefit will be phased out over 2030-32, but is still a solid income for the company at the moment.

Valuation and peer performance

Net income and free cash flow were more or less a leading indicator for the stock price. However, this has not been the case in the last 6 months. A gap has arisen between the stock price and free cash flow. In my previous case, I deemed that Enphase was undervalued as the free cash flow would likely recover due to the solar multi-year tailwind. Now the valuation has increasingly become attractive since the free cash flow rebounded and the stock price fell further.

As a result, the stock is currently trading at a very low price to a free cash flow multiple of 10.7x. Net income is still recovering after a negative quarter in 2024 and that's why the P/E ratio is trading at a higher multiple of 33x. Additionally, the company pays out stock-based compensation (a non-cash cost), that influences the gap between net income and free cash flow. To avoid dilution of existing shareholders, the company repurchases its own shares in the open market at favorable prices. The balance sheet can support more share buybacks in the coming quarters with a current ratio of 1.9x and a debt-to-equity ratio of 1.4x.

Furthermore, Enphase is the only cash-generating company from this list of 8 large solar companies. The EV to free cash flow ratio is a valuation metric that measures a company's total value against the cash it generates that is free to be used for dividends, stock buybacks, or debt repayment. This gives Enphase extra flexibility for strategic decisions and survivability until the market environment fully recovers.

Risk

The "one big beautiful bill" act will send shockwaves through the residential solar market if the bill goes live in its current form. The bill is expected to cut the 30% investment tax credit for residential solar projects by the end of 2025. The scope of its impact should be visible rather quickly. However, the Senate might ease these measures as Sen. Kevin Cramer said: "The Senate's cuts to the IRA may ultimately be a little more generous than the House's cuts".

Again in the long run I am not convinced this will stop the solar adoption. Globally, residential solar will continue to grow and renewed demand for high-quality products will return. In addition, a rough market environment could open up new acquisition opportunities for Enphase.

Takeaway

Enphase's valuation is nearing multi-year lows as strong profitability remains despite the decline in sales. The company increased efforts at reducing operational expenditures in the last two years. Those measures include substantial workforce reductions, limited discretionary spending, and the streamlining of its manufacturing footprint.

The demand for residential solar products is likely to rise sooner or later due to the cheap cost and the independency of energy. It is still a large initial investment but a cost-friendly source in the long term.

The disconnect between a cash-generating business and a depressed stock price has favorably increased the risk-reward ratio for either new investors or investors that are averaging down on their position. The upside potential is significant, as I expect the valuation gap to close when the market re-prices the stock based on its resilient free cash flow and market-leading margins. I keep my "Strong Buy" rating on Enphase at $43 a share.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Vinci: A Classic Value Play That's No Longer Undervalued
data.description:
Half a year ago, I wrote my initial analysis on Vinci SA (OTCPK:VCISF) (OTCPK:VCISY), highlighting that this underfollowed company was a bargain trading at just 8.86x P/FCF.

Since then, shares have appreciated by 47.63% and the total return has been 52.46%. Needless to say, that's a great return for just 6 months.

If I were to follow in the footsteps of other analysts, at this point, I'd give myself a pat on the back, highlighting what a genius I am. But that would be false praise.

At the time of writing the article, I expected Vinci to generate low-double digit returns, given the undervaluation and stable business model.

I didn't anticipate that Vinci would start skyrocketing right after I clicked "publish". I also didn't expect the USD to decline rapidly against the EUR, adding to the returns.

While a quick re-rating sometimes happens, that's the exception, not the norm when it comes to value investing. And that's one reason why many investors struggle to generate market-beating returns over the long term: they're not willing to wait, even though time will pass anyway.

Value investing, done right, has been proven to outperform other investing strategies over the long term, as I've experienced myself over the decades. But in order to benefit, we have to be greedy when others are fearful, and fearful when others are greedy.

Vinci's P/E ratio of 14.83 still looks relatively attractive compared to many other choices the markets offer us these days. But the risks I've described in my initial analysis are still there. Essentially, the outlook hasn't changed dramatically since I first covered the company; all that has changed is the price we're paying for the shares.

Momentum investors may scratch their heads, wondering why I'm downgrading Vinci to a "hold", at a time when the shares finally started moving, but if you're a rational investor, it makes sense.

The higher the valuation you pay for Vinci, the more risks you face and the lower your expected returns will be. It's not rocket science, yet many investors seem to struggle with this simple concept, especially in the current exuberant market environment.

Vinci has reached its fair value and as a value investor, who demands a margin of safety, I won't add to my position. Neither am I selling my shares, as I expect the company to keep generating significant free cash flows for the foreseeable future.

Vinci is a Consistent Free Cash Flow Generator

As a long-term investor, I tend to focus on the bigger picture, instead of sweating over quarterly earnings misses. To find great long-term compounders at attractive valuations, I run stock screeners all the time.

My focus is on consistent profitability and low P/FCF ratios. The screener usually shows thousands of stocks, and after careful analysis, less than 1% make it into my personal portfolio. Vinci is such a candidate.

With its "boring" business model, Vinci doesn't make headlines. You won't find any TikTokers shouting the stock is going to the moon. Yet, over the decades, the company has generated consistent returns.

The all-important FCF/Share has been rising quite nicely over the last 10 years, growing by 8.52% a year. As a long-term investor, the only question we need to ask is: will it continue?

As a shareholder, I'd like to see Vinci continue growing at this pace, but it's unlikely.

The first risk is French politicians, who are notorious for putting additional taxes on large corporations like Vinci. But the greater threat is the expiring toll road concessions, as the end of their life is coming closer now.

Vinci's Expiring Concessions Are A Concern

Twenty years ago, the company acquired toll road concessions in France at very attractive valuations. They've got built-in annual toll increases that are linked to the Consumer Price Index (CPI), and offer relatively stable cash flows for us investors. The French toll roads account for roughly 40% of Vinci's annual free cash flow, and they'll mostly expire during 2032-2036. That's an issue.

If Vinci will be able to extend the concessions is far from certain. But it's certain that a potential extension would be costlier than it was twenty years ago. Assets — especially reliable cash flow generators like infrastructure — are in high demand, and it shows in elevated valuations.

Vinci is trying its best to replace the expiring concessions, but the valuations paid, so far, leave me skeptical that the company can achieve its goal.

For a 55-year-long concession of the Budapest airport, the company paid roughly 20x EBITDA. The Edinburgh airport was slightly cheaper at 15x EBITDA. The most expensive acquisition was the Denver Northwest Parkway toll road, a concession that lasts 80 years, and Vinci thought was worth 67x EBITDA. An insane price.

In my previous analysis, I calculated that Vinci could replace roughly 50% to 67% of the expiring concessions with their free cash flow after dividends. This assumes that they keep paying 15 to 20x EBITDA for their acquisitions.

With those numbers in mind, let's do a simplified calculation of how much growth in FCF/share could be achieved until 2037, at the time the largest toll road has expired.

Can Vinci Keep Growing Even With Expiring Concessions?

For this simplified calculation, we're going to use conservative assumptions. We're going to assume 4% growth (roughly in line with inflation) in FCF/share until 2036 and a one-time reduction of 20% in that year.

The 20% reduction comes from the expiring concessions (roughly 40% of FCF) that were partly replaced (50%) by new concessions by then.

Given the higher growth rates achieved during the last decade, a 4% growth rate might seem too conservative, but after a decade of easy money policies, I'd like to factor in a period of lower growth for the coming decade.

I am well aware this calculation isn't as accurate as it could be — since the concessions will expire from 2032 to 2036 and new concessions will be acquired long before that, it also doesn't include potential buybacks, but it's close enough for us to determine the company's potential given the threat of the expiring concessions.

Year: FCF/Share (in Euro):
2024 11.80
2025 12.27
2026 12.76
2027 13.27
2028 13.80
2029 14.36
2030 14.93
2031 15.53
2032 16.15
2033 16.80
2034 17.47
2035 18.17
2036 18.89
2037 15.72

Given those conservative assumptions, FCF/share will rise from €11.80 in 2024 to €15.72 in 2037. That's a CAGR of just 2.23%. After the expiration of the toll roads, I expect Vinci to keep returning to higher growth rates.

Vinci will likely face slowing growth given the challenges, that's why it's important to stay disciplined and only buy into it whenever valuations are low. Right now, at 14.83x earnings and 10.56x P/FCF, the shares seem fully priced, and I downgrade it to a "hold".

Conclusion

Vinci remains a high-quality company with a proven track record of generating reliable free cash flow, but we're no longer looking for a bargain. The stock is fairly valued, as there are meaningful challenges on the horizon.

The market has quickly caught up to what I saw months ago, and from here, future returns will likely be in line with the markets, no longer offering a margin of safety.

I'll continue to hold my position, collect the dividends, and monitor the company's progress in replacing its expiring concessions. But I won't be adding more at current valuations.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Spending Stalls As U.S. Consumers Feel More Pressure
data.description:
By James Knightley

Anxious households become more reluctant to spend

Real consumer spending fell 0.3% month-on-month in May versus expectations of a 0.1% gain. Following big downward revisions to first quarter consumer spending growth yesterday, this is concerning. The MoM changes so far this year have been: January -0.6%, February -0.1%, March +0.7%, April +0.1% and May -0.3%, so the only decent report was March when people went and bought cars to get ahead of the 25% tariff hike. That means spending in real terms has fallen 0.2% since the start of the year. Consumer spending is 70% of the economy and if that is no longer contributing to growth, we have got a problem.

In terms of the outlook, consumer confidence is weak. Households are anxious about what tariff-induced price hikes will do to their spending power, while concerns about the robustness of the jobs market are on the rise. The volatility in household wealth has also been significant. Equity markets have recovered and are at all-time highs, but house prices nationally are starting to come under downward pressure thanks to rising inventory of properties for sale. Housing is where the bulk of middle America’s wealth is stored. Even if the One Big Beautiful Bill is delivered, the bulk of the tax cuts are merely an extension of the 2017 Tax Cuts and Jobs Act, meaning there is little new fiscal impetus to support household incomes.

Level of real consumer spending Dec 2024 = 100

Inflation fears to keep the Fed on hold until at least September

At the same time, incomes fell 0.4% MoM. That is because of weaker farm income and one-time social security payments to public sector workers have ended - they had boosted incomes markedly in March and April. Inflation is muted at 0.1% MoM, but that was expected. In our view, the inflation numbers are going to start rising 0.4% MoM imminently due to tariffs.

That is likely to keep the Federal Reserve somewhat cautious on cutting interest rates in the very near term – they want to make sure any inflation is temporary and not more permanent, such as the post-pandemic period when inflation kept on rising all the way up to 9%. We think we are in a very different jobs and wages environment to 2021/2022 and energy costs remain contained even after the Iran bombing raids, so we agree that inflation will be short-lived. However, the Fed will be wary given the stinging criticism it faced after saying 2021 inflation would be “transitory” when it turned out to be much more persistent. As such, we think the Fed will be on hold until at least September, but the likelihood of meaningful rate cuts is growing.

Content Disclaimer

This publication has been prepared by ING solely for information purposes irrespective of a particular user's means, financial situation or investment objectives. The information does not constitute investment recommendation, and nor is it investment, legal or tax advice or an offer or solicitation to purchase or sell any financial instrument. Read more.

Original Post

data.title: AMG GW&K Core Bond ESG Fund Q1 2025 Commentary
data.description:
The AMG GW&K Core Bond ESG Fund (MUTF:MBGVX) (Class N ) returned 2.66% in the first quarter of 2025, compared with a return of 2.78% for its benchmark, the Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate Bond Index. For the 12 months ending March 31, 2025, the Fund returned 4.32% versus 4.88% for the benchmark. Please note that this Fund has multiple share classes.

Market Overview

The first quarter offered a reminder of how quickly market narratives can shift. The period began with continued optimism around the strength of the U.S. economy, underpinned by a solid labor market, and resilient consumer. However, midway through the quarter, the emergence of softer economic data and fluctuating tariff policies drove market sentiment toward a heightened sense of apprehension around the growth and inflation outlook. Investors sought the safe haven of fixed income, as the market quickly shifted its focus to recession fears and global trade tensions. Throughout this shift, the Federal Reserve Board (the Fed) held rates steady, as expected, while signaling a more cautious growth and worrisome inflation outlook. The market, though, remains even more bearish on the economy, pricing in three cuts by the end of 2025 versus the Fed projecting just two.

The Bloomberg Aggregate Bond Index delivered a solid 2.8% return in the first quarter, recovering most of the ground it lost in the fourth quarter. The market's shift to a risk-off stance drove investors into bonds, pushing the belly of the curve lower. In January, the 10-year Treasury (US10Y) yield briefly hit 4.8%, its highest level since October 2023, before rallying 60 basis points to end the quarter 37 basis points lower. Similarly, the 2-year Treasury (US2Y) yield fell 36 basis points, leaving the 2 s -to10s yield curve little changed. Ultra-short rates, by contrast, held relatively steady during a bull flattening of the 3mos-to-10s yield curve, which ended the quarter back in negative territory, where it had been for the past two and a half years.

Positive returns in the corporate credit market masked the abrupt shift in sentiment. Investment grade and high-yield spreads rose by 14 and 60 basis points, respectively. Both segments delivered negative excess returns as their carry advantage was not enough to offset the impact of wider spreads. Driven by softer economic readings and concerns about tariff effects, lower-rated securities uniformly underperformed across the corporate credit market. CCC-rated securities highlighted the risk-off move as the only quality bucket to deliver a negative total return. Despite the more cautious risk outlook, many factors that have made the corporate bond sector appealing remain in place. Market dynamics are still healthy, with economic conditions supporting robust corporate credit fundamentals, and attractive all-in yields driving investor demand.

The securitized sector was not immune to the broader risk-off move as it underperformed comparable duration Treasuries. Growing concerns around potential changes to the Government Sponsored Enterprises' (GSEs) regulatory framework and the risk of privatization led to negative excess returns for agency mortgage-backed securities and overwhelmed the positive effects of a favorable technical environment and limited rate volatility. Within MBS, performance was mixed as lower coupon securities underperformed mid-to-higher coupon bonds, which delivered strong excess returns. Asset-backed securities (ABS) lagged, primarily due to weakness in the utility sector, which continues to face pressures related to California wildfires. In sympathy with growing macro uncertainty, consumer-facing ABS spreads also moved wider. Despite these challenges, we remain constructive on the securitized sector. MBS spreads are attractive relative to historical averages, while ABS finds support from strong underlying collateral performance and structural protections.

Performance Review

The Fund slightly lagged the Bloomberg Aggregate Bond Index for the quarter. An overweight to, and allocation within, securitized was a positive contributor. More specifically, our overweight to higher coupon specified pools within MBS was the largest positive contributor. Security selection was modestly positive and mostly coming from within BBB-rated corporates. This was offset by our general overweight to spread product amidst the sell-off in risk. The effects from duration and yield-curve positioning were modest, but ultimately negative.

Outlook and Positioning

As we enter the second quarter, the economy is experiencing an uncommonly pronounced divergence between soft and hard economic data. Our belief is that policy uncertainty has caused a shift in the balance of risks to the downside as consumer confidence and business sentiment have weakened. Current U.S. economic fundamentals remain sound on the back of a resilient labor market and rising incomes, but a consumer pull-back would lead to slower growth ahead. That said, the Fed has conveyed a willingness to ease policy in the interest of pursuing its dual mandate, so any deterioration in fundamentals is likely to be brief and shallow.

Our duration stance is neutral, as the upward pressure of rising inflation expectations is offset by emerging evidence of some real growth concerns in the data. We believe rates should remain relatively range bound until the market gains direction from either the hard or soft economic data, and further clarity around new trade and tariff policies is received.

Tactically, we are somewhat more cautious around spread sectors in this risk-off environment. Toward that end, we maintain our higher-quality bias within corporates, favoring more defensive sectors, while avoiding lower-rated cyclicals. High-quality securitized holdings, including agency MBS and ABS, continue to represent an important part of our overall allocation. During this more volatile period, we stay grounded in our long-term core process and will continue to stay vigilant as we navigate through this uncertain economic period.

The views expressed represent the opinions of GW&K Investment Management as of March 31, 2025, are not intended as a forecast or guarantee of future results, and are subject to change without notice.

There can be no guarantee that active management will produce the desired result.

The issuers of the bonds may not be able to meet interest or principal payments when the bonds come due.

The Fund is subject to the risks associated with investments in debt securities, such as default risk and fluctuations in the perception of the debtor's ability to pay its creditors.

Changing interest rates may adversely affect the value of an investment. An increase in interest rates typically causes the value of bonds and other fixed income securities to fall.

To the extent that the Fund invests in asset-backed or mortgage-backed securities, its exposure to prepayment and extension risks may be greater than investments in other fixed income securities.

Active and frequent trading of a fund may result in higher transaction costs and increased tax liability.

The Fund may invest in derivatives such as options and futures; the complexity and rapidly changing structure of derivatives markets may increase the possibility of market losses.

The Fund is subject to the risks associated with investments in emerging markets, such as erratic earnings patterns, economic and political instability, changing exchange controls, limitations on repatriation of foreign capital and changes in local governmental attitudes toward private investment, possibly leading to nationalization or confiscation of investor asset.

Investments in international securities are subject to certain risks of overseas investing including currency fluctuations and changes in political and economic conditions, which could result in significant market fluctuations. These risks are magnified in emerging markets.

Market prices of investments held by the Fund may fall rapidly or unpredictably due to a variety of economic or political factors, market conditions, disasters or public health issues, or in response to events that affect particular industries or companies.

Refer to the prospectus at AMG Wealth Platform to see the full list of applicable fund risks.

Applying the Fund's ESG investment criteria may result in the selection or exclusion of securities of certain issuers for reasons other than performance, and the Fund may underperform funds that do not utilize an ESG investment strategy. The application of this strategy may affect the Fund's exposure to certain companies, sectors, regions, countries or types of investments, which could negatively impact the Fund's performance depending on whether such investments are in or out of favor. Applying ESG criteria to investment decisions is qualitative and subjective by nature, and there is no guarantee that the criteria utilized by the Subadvisor or any judgment exercised by the Subadvisor will reflect the beliefs or values of any particular investor.

Many bonds have call provisions which allow the debtors to pay them back before maturity. This is especially true with mortgage securities, which can be paid back anytime. Typically debtors prepay their debt when it is to their advantage (when interest rates drop making a new loan at current rates more attractive), and thus likely to the disadvantage of bondholders, who may have to reinvest prepayment proceeds in securities with lower yields. Prepayment risk will vary depending on the provisions of the security and current interest rates relative to the interest rate of the debt.

A basis point refers to a common unit of measure for interest rates and other percentages in finance. One basis point is equal to 1/100 th of 1%, or 0.01% ( 0.0001 ), and is used to denote the percentage change in a financial instrument.

data.title: AMD: Let Your Profit Run
data.description:
AMD stock: previous thesis and new developments

I last wrote on Advanced Micro Devices, Inc. (NASDAQ:AMD) about 2 months ago on April 29, 2025. That article was titled "AMD Q1 Preview: Inflection Point Approaching (Technical Analysis).” As stated in the title, it served as a preview of its FQ1 earnings report (ER) and argued for a Strong Buy rating for the following considerations:

Trading patterns in the past 3~4 weeks suggest an imminent trend reversal for AMD stock prices. Technical indicators such as bullish crossover and rising RSI suggest increasing upward momentum and buying interest. I further expect AMD's upcoming FQ1 earnings report (ER, scheduled on May 6, 2025) to provide the trigger for such a reversal. I expect the ER to report profit margin expansion and continued growth in its key products.

The stock has indeed delivered a large price return of over 50% since then, outpacing both the broader tech sector and also the semiconductor sub-sector, as seen in the next chart below. Usually, a working bullish thesis tends to defeat itself. As prices advance, risks heighten.

But in the case of AMD, I have seen new catalysts around the stock since my last writing. The top two catalysts on my list are the developments of its new products (in particular the MI350 and MI400 accelerators) and also the changes in geopolitical risks (in particular the trade progress China and the U.S. have made). In the remainder of this follow-up article, I will argue why these new catalysts can offset the price advancements thus far to a large degree. As such, I believe this is the time to let your profit run and keep seeing the stock as a compelling buy opportunity.

AMD stock: Q1 recap and growth catalysts

AMD started FY 2025 on a strong foot, and I expect strong double-digit growth for the full fiscal year. In the Data Center segment, the company reported 57% year-over-year growth in the first quarter. The growth was mostly fueled by the expanding EPYC CPU deployments. I am optimistic that such growth momentum can be sustained thanks to its partnerships with hyperscalers and enterprise customers. In addition, with the new platforms and ROCm software advances reported in the ER, I see AMD’s position in the AI infrastructure space further reinforced.

In other segments, I see encouraging developments too, in particular in terms of AI-related offerings. For instance, the Client segment reported a 68% YOY revenue increase in Q1. The key drivers include strong desktop and notebook demand, higher ASPs, and growing traction in commercial PCs with Ryzen AI chips. In the Embedded system, I see demand gradually improving and believe the new edge AI offerings (such as the EPYC Embedded 9005 and Versal AI Edge) as key drivers.

All told, consensus expects 17% YOY EPS growth for the full fiscal 2025 as seen in the table below. Looking further out, consensus is also expecting EPS to accelerate in the next few years, with more than 47% YOY EPS growth in FY2026. Under these growth projections, the stock is quite reasonably valued in my view, with an FY1 P/E of 37x and FY2 P/E of 25x. Next, I will explain why I see good reasons behind such a seemingly aggressive forecast.

AMD stock: AI momentum in focus

As just mentioned in the forgoing section, a key factor behind my bullish view is the progress AMD has reported in AI chips and infrastructure. In particular, I believe its upcoming MI350 and MI400 accelerators can have a real chance of rivaling Nvidia's (NVDA) next-generation AI chips (like the B100) in terms of both real-world performance and efficiency. Currently, Nvidia’s AI chips still have a wide edge over AMD products in my view. Besides the differences in hardware specifications, a key advantage is NVDA’s CUDA software platform, which makes it easier for developers to use and deeply embedded in the AI ecosystem.

AMD’s recent acquisition of ZT Systems is very likely to change this in my assessment. As updated in its Q1 ER (see the earnings presentation below), ZT Systems offers rack-scale system capabilities and can help AMD to offer full rack-level AI solutions. I expect these new capabilities to help broaden the software adoption for AMD products significantly in the years to come.

Other risks and final thoughts

A key uncertainty that has been evolving since my last writing was the trade conflicts between the U.S. and its trading partners, especially China. AMD has sizable exposure to the China market.

To wit, the donut chart below illustrates its revenue share across various global regions. The U.S. accounts for the largest portion of revenue, representing 33.71%. China follows as a close second-largest contributor at 24.17%. Singapore makes up 14.02% of the revenue share, followed by Taiwan, Europe, Japan, et al. As such, when the tariff disputes first started in April, the market was rightly fearful as it can be difficult to assess the impact on AMD. My own worst scenario estimate is around a $2 billion revenue impact in FY 2025 alone from China's export controls on MI308 GPUs at the peak of the U.S.-China tariff conflicts.

However, the latest developments have led me to reconsider my worst-scenario estimate, which, I believe, is too pessimistic. Since my last writing, the U.S. and China have first agreed on a tariff truce in May, based on which a trade deal has been just finalized (see the news report for details below).

Seeking Alpha News: U.S. and China finalize trade deal; White House eyes agreements with 10 key partners, commerce secretary says. The U.S. and China have finalized a trade truce reached last month, with Beijing agreeing to supply rare earths in exchange for the lifting of U.S. countermeasures. The China agreement outlines the terms discussed in this year's trade negotiations between Beijing and Washington—a significant step after both sides previously accused each other of breaching earlier informal deals.

This development is very positive for AMD (and high-tech companies in general) in my view for at least two reasons. First, the deal could serve as a template for the U.S. to finalize trade agreements with other countries too and thus helping to speed up the resolution of the trade conflicts with other countries as well. Second, with Beijing resuming the supply of rare earth elements to the U.S., I expect the tech sector to resume its growth mode as these elements are crucial for many advanced products.

To close, this follow-up article on AMD was triggered by two new developments since my last analysis: its new product offerings and the progress China and the U.S. have made in resolving their trade conflicts. My assessment is that these catalysts offset a large degree of the stock price appreciation since my last writing, thus supporting a buy rating. My own journey has taught me that successful investment depends on a clear separation of long-term and near-term issues. That is why our Investing Group Service (Envision Early Retirement) monitors both in our stock recommendations.

In the case of AMD, it’s likely that new trade conflicts can surface and its new products (in particular the MI400 accelerator) can suffer unexpected issues as many other new products. But looking past these near-term issues, I see a company well-positioned to benefit from the secular growth of our IT and AI applications for years to come.

data.title: Counterfactuals And Revisionist History
data.description:
Here are some things I think I am thinking about.

1) Tariff counterfactuals

The stock market has returned to where it was before the whole tariff fiasco happened. And now I am seeing people say, “See, the tariff worries were all overblown!” The endgame here played out more or less the way I said it would back when I pegged 70% odds of “Scenario 2” in my April 19th note. Trump mostly caved on China and the effective tariff rate has now dropped to a rate that is still high, but nothing remotely close to the embargo-style levels we had in mid-April. And the markets have rightly bounced back. But that doesn’t mean there was no damage done.

When we assess the impact here, we have to look at it in relative trade terms. For instance, the US dollar has fallen 6.5% since the beginning of the year, when more and more protectionist measures were implemented. Some people might say a falling dollar is a good thing, but I don’t see it that way. You would never look at a collapsing currency in a place like Argentina and say, “Oh look, they’re more competitive now!” They technically are, but their currency collapsed because they have hyperinflation and their economy is less competitive on a relative basis. Likewise, it’s been a good thing that the USD was rising for so long, because it’s a coincident indicator that the USA is doing better in relative terms. We want our currency to rise because it’s a symbol that there’s strong demand for our currency and that we’re doing better on relative terms.

This is most evident in relative stock prices this year. While the US stock market is back to where it was before the whole tariff thing started, foreign stocks are now up 19% year to date. This is a sign that the markets expect the rest of the world to become more competitive in relative terms. Which is exactly what you’d expect from a policy like tariffs, which hurt our ability to compete on the global stage. This doesn’t mean we’re doing badly. It means we’re not doing as well as we otherwise would be. Which is exactly why economists call tariffs the “invisible tax”. Your economy might do just fine in absolute terms, but you don’t see that you’re doing worse in relative terms in large part because you don’t see the counterfactual outcome. It’s very likely that, if we hadn’t done any of this tariff stuff, the USA would be the blue line on that chart.

Anyhow, it’s a good thing that this isn’t causing widespread absolute damage to the US economy. But that doesn’t mean it isn’t causing relative damage to the US economy.

2) Why isn’t the Fed cutting rates?

JD Vance asked a good question on Twitter:

“I’d love to hear an argument for why Powell cut rates 50 points right before an election but can’t do it now with inflation lower.”

Trump is very eager to cut rates to stimulate growth. And I don’t disagree with him. I think inflation has come back essentially to target, and that the labor market is showing enough fragility that you could easily get rates down to 3.5% or so and feel okay about how loose policy is. And let’s not forget that housing, despite prices being somewhat robust, has been in the doldrums for years now. Most importantly, with inflation at 2.1% on headline PCE and 2.5% on core, I think the Fed can pretty much declare victory. Sure, it’s not exactly at their 2% target, but it’s below the historical average and close enough to target.

But this isn’t the slam dunk argument that it was a year ago when they cut 50. When the Fed cut 0.5% last September, the inflation rate was also 2.1%, but the rate of inflation had been on a one-way trend from 7.2% down to 2.1%. There had been almost no signs of a change in the downward trend, and so, it was pretty clear that a 5%+ rate was too restrictive relative to 2.1% inflation. And so, they cut 1% over the course of the next few quarters, and now the Fed Funds Rate is 4.1%. But the problem now is that inflation expectations and inflation itself have been mostly sideways for the last few quarters. And it had been trending from 2.1% to 2.6% over the 5-month period since September. So the worry was that their cutting was sparking a little inflation. And then, of course, we got the big tariff scare. And although the Fed said it wasn’t likely to cause sustained inflation, I can see why they would sit on their hands just to be sure. After all, 4.1% is restrictive, but it’s not nearly as restrictive as 5.1%.

The bottom line is, I am not especially worried about inflation at present. And I generally agree with the White House that the Fed should be cutting further. But I can also understand the Fed’s position that it’s okay to wait and see. After all, it’s not like the economy is cratering or anything. There’s no need to be overly zealous in either direction. So I can see both sides of the argument here, even though I lean towards a moderate pace of further cuts.

Well, that’s all I got for now. I’ve written about a billion words in the last few months and I am tired, so excuse me if I stop here. I’ll be in better form shortly. Until then, thanks for reading and stay disciplined.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: HIMZ: Buy For Short-Term Gains On Hims & Hers Health, But Be Wary Of The Risks
data.description:
Introduction

The Hims and Hers Health Inc Daily Target 2x Long ETF (NASDAQ:HIMZ) is a 2x leveraged fund that aims to track the daily performance of HIMS, the popular personal health and hygiene company. HIMS provides a plethora of direct-to-consumer health and wellness products, and this ETF helps to magnify exposure to the underlying name. Below are some key facts of the fund, taken from the prospectus.

  • AUM: $102.3M

  • Expense Ratio: 1.31%

  • Average Volume: 1.9M

HIMS is already a very volatile name, with a beta of 2.1 so this fund helps investors gain even more leverage and volatility on top. This would be a good fund for quick trades for investors with a high-risk appetite—not for long-term buys and holds. The following section will review the underlying company, HIMS, in more detail.

Underlying Company Analysis

Just this week, there was a major announcement that HIMS is ending its partnership with Novo Nordisk, which was centered around their weight loss drugs. This partnership was merely months in the making, so this stumped investors and led to a massive dip on the day (20%+) for HIMS stock. The CEO of HIMS, Andrew Dudum, expressed that the partnership was ended due to disagreements over patient practices.

Of course, this news is jarring for investors, as HIMS was rocked the day that it came out, but we believe that this could constitute a buying opportunity. In the next 2 trading days following the big dip, HIMS shares have rallied a bit, and are up ~6% since the major dip.

Since this company is frequently in the news, we think that big moves like this are the best time, in fact, to get into short-term plays via HIMZ.

The firm is also continuing to grow and operate on its terms, outside of this partnership ending. They announced their first Chief Product Officer, Dheerja Kaur from Robinhood, to develop and take to market more consumer-facing products. We believe that her experience, from launching Robinhood Gold, will be valuable in helping HIMS continually iterate on its portfolio and build out its global expansion and AI tools. Some of the products that are in the pipeline for HIMS include AI-powered tools in the therapy, nutrition, and self-coaching spaces, and these can all be catalysts for the firm to continue growing in the future.

In fact, the company is also looking to increase its share of the women’s personal health and wellness market, where before they were more focused on the male market. The firm revealed a new product this week that targets women who have hair loss, via a prescription regrowth gummy. This product helps to enable more hair growth and provides vitamins to boost growth, and it shows that the company is dedicated to expanding the overall end markets its products serve. See the following image from their May 2025 Investor Presentation for a glimpse at the various end markets HIMS is aiming to operate in.

We believe that this is a well-rounded healthcare company that makes it 1) cheaper and 2) less stigmatized for men and women to receive the care that they need, all from the comfort of their own homes. HIMS is building a holistic portfolio of wellness products that will reach numerous end markets and provide value to many segments of consumers.

This strategy appears to be working, as per their investor presentation, HIMS is truly starting to dominate the market in direct-to-consumer health and wellness, earning close to 50% share of total customers and total sales compared to its peers. We are confident in HIMS as a company and think that it will continue to have many catalysts in the future that will help move its share price.

Risks of Leverage

When it comes to leveraged ETFs, there are additional risks and return profiles beyond what one might see from a non-leveraged investment. One important concept is loss recoupment. We ask, “If I lose X% today, what percentage do I need to gain tomorrow to breakeven?”. This relationship looks like the below for all investments:

As you can see, the day 2 gain required to break even is always higher than the previous day’s loss. For example, if I lose 20% today, I need to gain 25% tomorrow in order to breakeven.

Also, the above line is exponential, meaning that the day 2 gain required grows faster than the day 1 loss. For a 20% day 1 loss, the day 2 gain needs to be 25%/20% = 1.25x. However, for a 40% day 1 loss, the day 2 gain needs to be 66.67%/40% = 1.67x.

The above relationship holds true for leveraged ETFs. However, it does not represent the full picture. Since leveraged ETF performance is fully reliant on the underlying equity (in this case, HIMS), it makes more sense to see this relationship from the lens of the underlying stock. So instead we ask: “If I own a 2x leveraged ETF on stock Y, and it loses X% today, how much does stock Y need to increase by tomorrow for me to breakeven on the ETF?” Let’s add that line to the chart:

In this chart, the blue line is the same as the previous chart and the red line represents the leveraged ETF version. As you can see, not only is it higher than the blue line, but it grows faster than the blue line too. This means that for leveraged ETFs, the gain required for breakeven is larger than for non-leveraged investments and the gain required grows faster.

20% Day 1 Loss

30% Day 1 Loss

Day 2 gain required for standard investment

25% (1.25x)

42.86% (1.43x)

Day 2 gain required for 2x leveraged investment

50% (2x)

75% (2.5x)

We see that losses create a bigger impact than gains, and this is especially true for leveraged investments. As such, volatility is also a potential risk factor. If you owned HIMS directly, and it increased one week by 10% with every day being positive, you would get the same total return if it increased by the same 10% but one or more of the days was negative. Either way, you make 10%. However, for an ETF that is 2x leveraged on HIMS, your returns would be lower in the week with negative days even though the underlying stock had the same performance.

As the issuer of HIMZ, Defiance puts it on their page for HIMZ: “The Fund may not achieve investment results, before fees and expenses, that correspond to two times (2x) the daily performance of the underlying security, and may return substantially less during such periods.”

Conclusion

HIMS is a very volatile company—consistently in the news and consistently launching new products and services to a growing number of end users. This volatility is actually a benefit for investors in this leveraged fund, HIMZ, because of the short-term nature of this ETF. These wild swings and moves based on their various catalysts help to push this 2x leveraged fund to swing even more, and as a result, make this fund even more attractive to traders who prioritize short-term moves. This is definitely not a fund for buy-and-hold investors, as the volatility outweighs the benefits and gains, but for short-term traders looking for large movements on an already volatile name, this is a great fund.
